summary: Joel reports on a brief experiment with pair programming at Fog Creek Software. He finds it highly effective for sprinting through lists of small bug fixes, where it enables incredible velocity and catches typos and small errors immediately. However, he notes it's less suited for longer programming tasks that require deep concentration. He emphasizes that personal compatibility between partners is absolutely critical to making pair programming work.
key_points: Pair programming is the most memorable aspect of extreme programming, overshadowing other XP practices
Pair programming excels at sprinting through many small bug fixes with high velocity
The technique is less effective for longer tasks requiring sustained concentration
Personal synergy between programming partners is crucial to success
Fog Creek's no-jerks hiring policy is a prerequisite for collaborative practices like pairing
money_quotes: Actually pair programming, which is all anybody remembers about extreme programming.
It's very effective when you have a long list of small bug fixes you want to sprint through, because you can reach incredible velocity.
Typos and small bugs get caught right away.
You can't stop and concentrate, so it's probably not so useful for longer programming tasks.
It's like being a cop: if you don't like your partner, it's one of the less pleasant rings of hell!
Rule #0 in Fog Creek hiring is no jerks.
key_insight: Pair programming is a powerful technique for high-velocity bug fixing but requires both the right type of task and strong personal compatibility between partners to work well.
